summary: This lesson deepens the gustar construction by practicing it with le/les and introduces two more verbs that work the same way: interesar (to be interesting to) and parecer (to seem to). It also teaches poner (to put) and its -pose cognate pattern, the expression 'me parece bien', and using le to mean 'on it'.

teachings.summary: Extended gustar practice with third-person indirect objects: le gusta (he/she likes it), les gusta (they like it), les gustan (they like them). Emphasized translating as 'to be pleasing to' rather than 'to please' because it preserves the 'to' that maps to le/les.
teachings.rule: Store gustar as 'to be pleasing to' (not 'to please') so the 'to' maps naturally to le/les. Le gusta = it is pleasing to him/her. Les gustan = they are pleasing to them.

examples.english: We like it. (It is pleasing to us.)
He/she likes it. (It is pleasing to him/her.)

teachings.notes: Gustar uses le/les (not lo/la). Always start with the verb (gusta/gustan), then add the pronoun.

teachings.summary: Interesar works like gustar: translate as 'to be interesting to' rather than 'to interest'. Uses le/les for third person. Can be used with conditional: te interesaría (would it interest you).
teachings.rule: Me interesa = it interests me. Le interesa = it interests him/her. Les interesa = it interests them. Uses le/les, not lo/la.

examples.english: Is she interested in dancing tonight?

examples.spanish: ¿Le interesa salir a bailar esta noche?
examples.english: Is she interested in going out to dance tonight?
I would be interested in seeing it.

teachings.notes: Interesante (interesting) is the adjective. Interesar is the verb. Always check verb type before conjugating (-ar, -er, -ir).

teachings.summary: Parecer (to seem) works like gustar and interesar with le/les. 'Me parece' = it seems to me. 'Me parece que...' = it seems to me that... The 'que' (that) is obligatory in Spanish even though it's optional in English.
teachings.rule: Me parece = it seems to me. Me parece que + clause (que is obligatory). Me parece bien = it seems good/fine to me. Parecer is an -er verb.

examples.spanish: Me parece que no quiere salir esta noche.
examples.english: It seems to me that he doesn't want to go out tonight.

examples.spanish: Me parece que no le gusta.
examples.english: It seems to me that he doesn't like it.
It doesn't seem good to me.
It doesn't seem good to him/her.
Doesn't it seem ok to you (formal)?

teachings.notes: 'Me parece bien' uses bien (well) not bueno (good). There is a gray area between bien and bueno that you pick up through listening. The 'que' after parecer is obligatory in Spanish, unlike English where 'that' is optional. Spanish shows the that/what connection: que = that, qué = what, like English there/where, then/when.

teachings.summary: Spanish shows clearly that 'that' and 'what' are the same word (que/qué), just as English shows: that/what, there/where, then/when (remove t, add w).
teachings.rule: que = that (conjunction). qué = what (question). Same word, different function.

examples.spanish: Me parece que no quiere ir.
examples.english: It seems to me that he doesn't want to go.

teachings.notes: English parallel: that->what, there->where, then->when (replace t with w).

teachings.summary: Poner means to put. When English has -pose inside a verb, Spanish has -poner: compose=componer, suppose=suponer, oppose=oponer, impose=imponer. The com- in componer is actually con- (with), where n changes to m before p.
teachings.rule: English -pose = Spanish -poner. compose=componer, suppose=suponer, oppose=oponer, impose=imponer.

examples.english: I want to put it here.
I would like to put it here.

teachings.notes: Com- in componer is actually con- (with), where the n assimilates to m before p.

teachings.summary: Le can replace 'on it' or 'to it' when talking about putting something on something. 'Ponerle sal' = to put salt on it. Les for plural.
teachings.rule: Le replaces 'on it' with poner: ponerle sal = put salt on it. Ponerles sal = put salt on them.

examples.english: I want to put salt on it.
I would like to put salt on it.

examples.spanish: Me gustaría ponerle un poco más sal.
examples.english: I would like to put a little more salt on it.

examples.spanish: Me gustaría ponerles un poco más sal.
examples.english: I would like to put a little more salt on them.

teachings.summary: Un poco means 'a little'. Can be combined with más (more): un poco más = a little more.

vocabulary.english: to interest, to be interesting to
